title: Graphs & Networks
description: Network modeling in Python: Adjacency Lists, all graph operations, and pathfinding traversals (DFS & BFS).

sections.title: Graph Representations & Operations
sections.description: Graphs consist of vertices and edges. Key operations include Adding/Removing Vertices and Edges. Time complexities depend heavily on whether the graph is represented using an Adjacency List or Matrix.

sections.title: Depth-First Search (DFS) & Breadth-First Search (BFS)
sections.description: DFS and BFS are graph traversal operations, both running in O(V + E) time complexity (where V is vertices and E is edges).
sections.codeBlock:
# Simple adjacency list representation
graph = {
    'A': ['B', 'C'],
    'B': ['D', 'E'],
    'C': ['F'],
    'D': [],
    'E': ['F'],
    'F': []
}

# 1. Depth-First Search (DFS) - explore deep path before backtracking
def dfs(node, visited, path):
    if node not in visited:
        visited.add(node)
        path.append(node)
        for neighbor in graph[node]:
            dfs(neighbor, visited, path)

visited_dfs = set()
path_dfs = []
dfs('A', visited_dfs, path_dfs)
print(path_dfs)        # ['A', 'B', 'D', 'E', 'F', 'C']

# 2. Breadth-First Search (BFS) - explore neighbors level-by-level
from collections import deque
def bfs(start):
    visited = {start}
    queue = deque([start])
    path = []
    while queue:
        node = queue.popleft()
        path.append(node)
        for neighbor in graph[node]:
            if neighbor not in visited:
                visited.add(neighbor)
                queue.append(neighbor)
    return path

print(bfs('A'))        # ['A', 'B', 'C', 'D', 'E', 'F']

details:
Graph Representations: Graphs are represented either by Adjacency Matrices (2D arrays indicating edge connections) or Adjacency Lists. Adjacency lists are highly space-efficient for sparse graphs (graphs where the number of edges is much less than V^2), making dictionary-of-lists representations standard in Python.

Pathfinding Algorithms: BFS guarantees finding the shortest path between two nodes in an unweighted graph, making it perfect for peer-to-peer connection mapping. DFS is utilized in cycle detection, topological sorting (e.g. build system dependencies), and finding strongly connected components.
